title.zh: 卷三十七 季布欒布田叔傳
title.en: Volume 37: Ji Bu, Luan Bu and Tian Shu
title.raw: 卷三十七 季布欒布田叔傳 Volume 37: Ji Bu, Luan Bu and Tian Shu

sentences.zh: 季布，楚人也，為任俠有名。

translations.literal: Ji Bu was a man of Chu; as one who practiced knight-errantry he had a famous name.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu was a native of Chu and had earned wide fame as a knight-errant.

sentences.zh: 項籍使將兵，數窘漢王。

translations.literal: Xiang Ji employed him to lead troops, and several times he put the King of Han in dire straits.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu put him in command of an army, and again and again he drove the King of Han into desperate positions.

sentences.zh: 項籍滅，高祖購求布千金，敢有舍匿，罪三族。

translations.literal: When Xiang Ji was destroyed, the High Ancestor offered a thousand catties of gold for Ji Bu and decreed that whoever dared conceal him would be guilty of a crime punishable by execution of three clans.
translations.idiomatic: After Xiang Yu fell, Gaozu posted a reward of a thousand pounds of gold for Ji Bu and announced that anyone who harbored him would face the three-clans penalty.

sentences.zh: 布匿濮陽周氏，周氏曰：「漢求將軍急，跡且至臣家，能聽臣，臣敢進計；

translations.literal: Ji Bu hid with the Zhou clan of Puyang. The Zhou said: "The Han court is urgently seeking the general; their traces will soon reach my house. If you can heed me, I dare advance a plan;
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu took refuge with a Mr. Zhou of Puyang. Zhou told him, "The Han are hunting you relentlessly; their agents will trace you here before long. If you will listen to me, I have a plan to propose;

sentences.zh: 即否，願先自剄。」

translations.literal: if not, I would rather first cut my own throat."
translations.idiomatic: if not, I would sooner slit my own throat first."

sentences.zh: 布許之。

translations.literal: Ji Bu assented.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu agreed.

sentences.zh: 乃髡鉗布，衣褐，置廣柳車中，并與其家僮數十人，之魯朱家所賣之。

translations.literal: Then they shaved his head and put the iron collar on him, clothed him in coarse hemp, placed him in a broad-willow hearse-cart, and together with several tens of household slaves of his family went to the place of the Zhu family of Lu to sell him.
translations.idiomatic: They shaved his head, clamped him in the cangue, dressed him in a beggar's robe, hid him in a covered funeral wagon, and with several dozen of his household slaves drove him to the Zhu household in Lu to be sold as a slave.

sentences.zh: 朱家心知其季布也，買置田舍。

translations.literal: Zhu Jia knew in his heart that he was Ji Bu, bought him, and placed him in a field house.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Jia recognized him as Ji Bu, purchased him, and lodged him on a farm.

sentences.zh: 乃之雒陽見汝陰侯滕公，說曰：「季布何罪？

translations.literal: Then he went to Luoyang to see the Marquis of Ruyin, Lord Teng, and urged him, saying: "What crime has Ji Bu committed?
translations.idiomatic: He then traveled to Luoyang to see the Marquis of Ruyin, Xiahou Ying, and argued, "What offense has Ji Bu committed?

sentences.zh: 臣各為其主用，職耳。

translations.literal: Ministers each served their own masters in their use—it was their office.
translations.idiomatic: Each minister served his own lord as duty required; that was simply the nature of the office.

sentences.zh: 項氏臣豈可盡誅邪？

translations.literal: Could all the ministers of the Xiang clan be put to death?
translations.idiomatic: You cannot execute every minister who once served the house of Xiang.

sentences.zh: 今上始得天下，而以私怨求一人，何示不廣也！

translations.literal: Now the sovereign has just obtained the empire yet seeks one man out of private resentment—what narrowness of spirit does that display!
translations.idiomatic: The Son of Heaven has only just won the realm, yet he hunts one man for a private grudge—what a cramped message that sends to the world!

sentences.zh: 且以季布之賢，漢求之急如此，此不北走胡，南走越耳。

translations.literal: Moreover, given Ji Bu's worth and the Han court's pursuit of him with such urgency, he will not flee north to the Hu nor south to Yue for nothing.
translations.idiomatic: A man of Ji Bu's caliber, hounded this fiercely, will surely flee north to the Xiongnu or south to Yue.

sentences.zh: 夫忌壯士以資敵國，此伍子胥所以鞭荊平之墓也。

translations.literal: To drive a brave man into the arms of an enemy state—this is why Wu Zixu whipped the tomb of King Ping of Jing.
translations.idiomatic: Alienating a stalwart and strengthening a rival is how King Ping of Chu earned having his grave flogged by Wu Zixu.

sentences.zh: 君何不從容為上言之？」

translations.literal: Why do you not at your leisure speak of this to the sovereign?"
translations.idiomatic: Why will you not find a suitable moment and put this to the throne?"

sentences.zh: 滕公心知朱家大俠，意布匿其所，乃許諾。

translations.literal: Lord Teng knew in his heart that Zhu Jia was a great knight-errant and suspected Ji Bu was hidden with him, and so he promised.
translations.idiomatic: Lord Teng knew Zhu Jia was a famous knight-errant and guessed Ji Bu lay concealed with him, yet he gave his word.

sentences.zh: 侍間，果言如朱家指。

translations.literal: When he waited on an interval of opportunity, he indeed spoke as Zhu Jia had directed.
translations.idiomatic: When he next attended the emperor, he spoke exactly as Zhu Jia had urged.

sentences.zh: 上乃赦布。

translations.literal: The sovereign then pardoned Ji Bu.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor thereupon granted Ji Bu an amnesty.

sentences.zh: 當是時，諸公皆多布能摧剛為柔，朱家亦以此名聞當世。

translations.literal: At that time the great men all praised Ji Bu for being able to bend what was rigid into what was supple, and Zhu Jia too by this became renowned in the age.
translations.idiomatic: Men of standing marveled that Ji Bu could turn inflexible steel into pliant silk, and Zhu Jia's name rang through the age for it.

sentences.zh: 布召見，謝，拜郎中。

translations.literal: Ji Bu was summoned to audience, apologized, and was appointed gentleman of the palace.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu was called to court, offered thanks, and was appointed a gentleman of the palace.

sentences.zh: 孝惠時，為中郎將。

translations.literal: In the time of Emperor Xiaohui he was general of the gentlemen of the palace.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Xiaohui he rose to general of the gentlemen of the palace.

sentences.zh: 單于嘗為書嫚呂太后，太后怒，召諸將議之。

translations.literal: The Chanyu once sent a letter insulting Empress Dowager Lü; the empress dowager was angry and summoned the generals to deliberate.
translations.idiomatic: The Chanyu once wrote a letter grossly insulting Empress Dowager Lü, who in her fury convened the generals for counsel.

sentences.zh: 上將軍樊噲曰：「臣願得十萬眾，橫行匈奴中。」

translations.literal: The senior general Fan Kuai said: "I wish to obtain a hundred thousand men and sweep through the midst of the Xiongnu."
translations.idiomatic: Fan Kuai, the senior general, declared, "Give me one hundred thousand troops and I will ride roughshod through the heart of the Xiongnu steppe."

sentences.zh: 諸將皆阿呂太后，以噲言為然。

translations.literal: The generals all fawned on Empress Dowager Lü and took Fan Kuai's words as correct.
translations.idiomatic: The generals to a man curried favor with the empress dowager and agreed with Fan Kuai.

sentences.zh: 布曰：「樊噲可斬也。

translations.literal: Ji Bu said: "Fan Kuai ought to be beheaded.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu replied, "Fan Kuai deserves execution.

sentences.zh: 夫以高帝兵三十餘萬，困於平城，噲時亦在其中。

translations.literal: For with the High Ancestor's army of more than three hundred thousand men trapped at Pingcheng, Fan Kuai was also then among them.
translations.idiomatic: The High Emperor led more than three hundred thousand soldiers to Pingcheng and was surrounded there—and Fan Kuai was in that army.

sentences.zh: 今噲奈何以十萬眾橫行匈奴中，面謾！

translations.literal: Now how can Fan Kuai with a hundred thousand men sweep through the midst of the Xiongnu—he insults you to your face!
translations.idiomatic: How, then, can the same Fan Kuai promise one hundred thousand men to rampage through the Xiongnu heartland? He is brazenly lying to your faces!

sentences.zh: 且秦以事胡，陳勝等起。

translations.literal: Moreover, because Qin devoted itself to the Hu, Chen Sheng and others rose.
translations.idiomatic: Besides, the Qin court exhausted the realm on the northern frontier, and Chen Sheng and his like rose in rebellion.

sentences.zh: 今瘡痍未瘳，噲又面諛，欲搖動天下。」

translations.literal: Now the wounds are not yet healed, and Fan Kuai again flatters to your faces, wishing to shake the empire."
translations.idiomatic: The realm is still lacerated and unhealed, and Fan Kuai would flatter you to your faces and unsettle the whole world again."

sentences.zh: 是時殿上皆恐，太后罷朝，遂不復議擊匈奴事。

translations.literal: At that time those in the hall were all afraid; the empress dowager ended court and did not again deliberate on attacking the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: The courtiers in the hall were stricken with fear; the empress dowager dismissed the session and never again discussed a strike against the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 布為河東守。

translations.literal: Ji Bu was governor of Hedong.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu served as governor of Hedong.

sentences.zh: 孝文時，人有言其賢，召欲以為御史大夫。

translations.literal: In the time of Emperor Xiaowen, someone spoke of his worth, and he was summoned with the intention of making him imperial clerk.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Xiaowen, men praised his talent, and the throne summoned him intending to appoint him imperial clerk.

sentences.zh: 人又言其勇，使酒難近。

translations.literal: Someone again spoke of his courage, that when drunk he was hard to approach.
translations.idiomatic: Others spoke of his ferocity and his habit of drinking himself into a temper no one could abide.

sentences.zh: 至，留邸一月，見罷。

translations.literal: When he arrived, he was lodged at the guesthouse a month and then dismissed without appointment.
translations.idiomatic: He reached the capital, was kept waiting a month in the official hostel, and was sent home without receiving any post.

sentences.zh: 布進曰：「臣待罪河東，陛下無故召臣，此人必有以臣欺陛下者。

translations.literal: Ji Bu advanced and said: "Your servant awaits punishment at Hedong; for no reason Your Majesty summoned me—there must be someone who deceived Your Majesty about me.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu stepped forward and said, "I stand guilty of whatever fault you find in my administration of Hedong; you summoned me without cause—someone must have misled you about my record.

sentences.zh: 今臣至，無所受事，罷去，此人必有毀臣者。

translations.literal: Now your servant has arrived, received no assignment, and been dismissed to leave—there must be someone who has slandered me.
translations.idiomatic: Now I have come, been given no charge, and dismissed—someone has surely slandered me.

sentences.zh: 夫陛下以一人譽召臣，一人毀去臣，臣恐天下有識者聞之，有以窺陛下。」

translations.literal: For Your Majesty to summon your servant on one man's praise and dismiss your servant on one man's slander—I fear that men of judgment in the realm, hearing this, will thereby peer into Your Majesty's depths."
translations.idiomatic: If a single voice of praise can fetch me and a single voice of blame can send me away, discerning men everywhere will infer how shallow your judgments are."

sentences.zh: 上默然，慚曰：「河東吾股肱郡，故特召君耳。」

translations.literal: The sovereign was silent, ashamed, and said: "Hedong is my arm-and-thigh commandery; therefore I specially summoned you."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor fell silent, then said with embarrassment, "Hedong is one of the sinews of the realm; I called you for that reason alone."

sentences.zh: 布之官。

translations.literal: Ji Bu went to his post.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu returned to his duties in Hedong.

sentences.zh: 辯士曹丘生數招權顧金錢，事貴人趙談等，與竇長君善。

translations.literal: The rhetorician Cao Qiusheng repeatedly solicited influence and sought money, served the noble Zhao Tan and others, and was on good terms with the elder Dou.
translations.idiomatic: The persuader Cao Qiusheng traded on connections and chased profit, curried favor with grandees such as Zhao Tan, and was close to Dou Zhang.

sentences.zh: 布聞，寄書諫長君曰：「吾聞曹丘生非長者，勿與通。」

translations.literal: When Ji Bu heard, he sent a letter remonstrating the elder Dou, saying: "I have heard that Cao Qiusheng is not a man of integrity; do not associate with him."
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu learned of it and wrote to Dou Zhang warning him, "Cao Qiusheng is no gentleman; break off dealings with him."

sentences.zh: 及曹丘生歸，欲得書請布。

translations.literal: When Cao Qiusheng returned, he wished to obtain a letter to request Ji Bu.
translations.idiomatic: When Cao Qiusheng went home, he wanted a letter of introduction to Ji Bu.

sentences.zh: 竇長君曰：「季將軍不說足下，足下無往。」

translations.literal: The elder Dou said: "General Ji is not pleased with you; you must not go."
translations.idiomatic: Dou Zhang said, "General Ji dislikes you; you had best not go."

sentences.zh: 固請書，遂行。

translations.literal: He firmly requested a letter, and then went.
translations.idiomatic: Cao pressed until Dou gave him a letter, then set out.

sentences.zh: 使人先發書，布果大怒，待曹丘。

translations.literal: He sent someone ahead to deliver the letter first; Ji Bu was indeed greatly angry and awaited Cao Qiu.
translations.idiomatic: He had a messenger deliver the letter in advance; Ji Bu was furious and waited for Cao in a rage.

sentences.zh: 曹丘至，則揖布曰：「楚人諺曰『得黃金百，不如得季布諾』，足下何以得此聲梁楚之間哉？

translations.literal: When Cao Qiu arrived, he bowed to Ji Bu and said: "The proverb of Chu says, 'To obtain a hundred catties of yellow gold is not as good as obtaining Ji Bu's promise'—how did you acquire such a reputation between Liang and Chu?
translations.idiomatic: When Cao arrived, he bowed and said, "Chu has a saying: 'A hundred pounds of gold cannot match one promise from Ji Bu.' How did you win such renown across Liang and Chu?

sentences.zh: 且僕與足下俱楚人，使僕游揚足下名於天下，顧不美乎？

translations.literal: Moreover, your servant and you are both men of Chu; were your servant to spread your name through the realm, would it not be fine?
translations.idiomatic: We are both sons of Chu; were I to trumpet your name to the world, would that not be splendid?

sentences.zh: 何足下距僕之深也！」

translations.literal: Why do you keep your servant at such a distance!"
translations.idiomatic: Why do you shut me out so coldly?"

sentences.zh: 布乃大說。

translations.literal: Ji Bu was then greatly pleased.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu's anger melted into delight.

sentences.zh: 引入，留數月，為上客，厚送之。

translations.literal: He led him in, kept him several months, treated him as a honored guest, and sent him off generously.
translations.idiomatic: He brought him inside, entertained him for months as an honored guest, and sent him away with rich gifts.

sentences.zh: 布名所以益聞者，曹丘揚之也。

translations.literal: The reason Ji Bu's name became still more heard was that Cao Qiu praised him.
translations.idiomatic: It was Cao Qiusheng's praise that made Ji Bu's fame resound even farther.

sentences.zh: 布弟季心氣蓋關中，遇人恭謹，為任俠，方數千里，士爭為死。

translations.literal: Ji Bu's younger brother Ji Xin's spirit covered Guanzhong; in meeting people he was respectful and careful, practiced knight-errantry, and over several thousand li knights vied to die for him.
translations.idiomatic: His younger brother Ji Xin dominated Guanzhong in reputation: courteous in person, a knight-errant whose name carried for a thousand miles, with fighting men ready to die at his call.

sentences.zh: 嘗殺人，亡吳，從爰絲匿，長事爰絲，弟畜灌夫、籍福之屬。

translations.literal: He once killed a man, fled to Wu, followed Yuan Ang into hiding, served Yuan Ang with the respect due an elder, and treated Guan Fu, Ji Fu, and their like as younger brothers.
translations.idiomatic: He had once killed a man and fled to Wu, where he took refuge with Yuan Ang, served him as a patron, and treated Guan Fu and Ji Fu almost as younger brothers.

sentences.zh: 嘗為中司馬，中尉郅都不敢加。

translations.literal: He once was central marshal; Commandant Zhi Du did not dare touch him.
translations.idiomatic: He held the post of central marshal; even Commandant Zhi Du would not provoke him.

sentences.zh: 少年多時時竊借其名以行。

translations.literal: Young men often secretly borrowed his name to act.
translations.idiomatic: Young blades constantly invoked his name to throw their weight around.

sentences.zh: 當是時，季心以勇，布以諾，聞關中。

translations.literal: At that time Ji Xin was heard of in Guanzhong for courage and Ji Bu for keeping his word.
translations.idiomatic: In those days Guanzhong spoke of Ji Xin for valor and Ji Bu for a promise as good as gold.

sentences.zh: 布母弟丁公，為項羽將，逐窘高祖彭城西。

translations.literal: Ji Bu's maternal younger brother Lord Ding was a general of Xiang Yu and pursued and cornered the High Ancestor west of Pengcheng.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu's uncle by marriage, Lord Ding, served Xiang Yu as a general and harried Gaozu west of Pengcheng.

sentences.zh: 短兵接，漢王急，顧謂丁公曰：「兩賢豈相厄哉！」

translations.literal: With short weapons engaged, the King of Han was in straits, looked back, and said to Lord Ding: "Would two worthy men afflict each other!"
translations.idiomatic: In close combat the King of Han was hard pressed, turned to Lord Ding, and cried, "Must two able men destroy each other?"

sentences.zh: 丁公引兵而還。

translations.literal: Lord Ding led his troops back.
translations.idiomatic: Lord Ding withdrew his troops.

sentences.zh: 及項王滅，丁公謁見高祖，以丁公徇軍中，曰：「丁公為項王臣不忠，使項王失天下者也。」

translations.literal: When King Xiang was destroyed, Lord Ding requested audience with the High Ancestor; taking Lord Ding he displayed him through the army, saying: "Lord Ding was a minister to King Xiang who was not loyal—he is the one who caused King Xiang to lose the empire."
translations.idiomatic: After Xiang Yu fell, Lord Ding came to Gaozu, who paraded him before the host and declared, "This man was Xiang Yu's minister yet betrayed him; he is the reason Xiang Yu lost the realm."

sentences.zh: 遂斬之，曰：「使後為人臣無傚丁公也！」

translations.literal: He then beheaded him, saying: "Let later generations of ministers not imitate Lord Ding!"
translations.idiomatic: He had him beheaded, adding, "Let no future minister take Lord Ding as his model!"

sentences.zh: 欒布，梁人也。

translations.literal: Luan Bu was a man of Liang.
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu came from Liang.

sentences.zh: 彭越為家人時，嘗與布游，窮困，賣庸於齊，為酒家保。

translations.literal: When Peng Yue was still a commoner, he once traveled with Bu; in poverty they sold their labor in Qi and worked as a tavern guarantor.
translations.idiomatic: While Peng Yue was still a commoner, he and Luan Bu had roamed together; destitute, they hired out as laborers in Qi and Luan tended bar in an inn.

sentences.zh: 數歲別去，而布為人所略，賣為奴於燕。

translations.literal: After several years they parted, and Bu was kidnapped by someone and sold as a slave in Yan.
translations.idiomatic: Years later they separated, and Luan Bu was seized and sold into slavery in Yan.

sentences.zh: 為其主家報仇，燕將臧荼舉以為都尉。

translations.literal: He avenged his master's household on their enemy, and the Yan general Zang Tu recommended him as commandant.
translations.idiomatic: He took revenge for his master's family, and the Yan general Zang Tu nominated him commandant.

sentences.zh: 荼為燕王，布為將。

translations.literal: When Tu became King of Yan, Bu was a general.
translations.idiomatic: When Zang Tu became king of Yan, Luan Bu served as his general.

sentences.zh: 及荼反，漢擊燕，虜布。

translations.literal: When Tu rebelled, Han attacked Yan and captured Bu.
translations.idiomatic: When Zang Tu revolted, the Han forces conquered Yan and took Luan Bu prisoner.

sentences.zh: 梁王彭越聞之，乃言上，請贖布為梁大夫。

translations.literal: King Liang Peng Yue heard of it and spoke to the sovereign, requesting to ransom Bu as a grandee of Liang.
translations.idiomatic: King Peng Yue of Liang petitioned the throne to ransom him and appoint him a grandee of Liang.

sentences.zh: 使於齊，未反，漢召彭越責以謀反，夷三族，梟首雒陽，下詔有收視者輒捕之。

translations.literal: He was sent on mission to Qi; before he returned, Han summoned Peng Yue and charged him with plotting rebellion, exterminated three clans, displayed the head at Luoyang, and issued an edict that whoever collected or viewed the corpse would be arrested at once.
translations.idiomatic: Luan was dispatched to Qi; before he could return, the court summoned Peng Yue, condemned him for treason, wiped out three generations of his kin, exposed his head in Luoyang, and decreed immediate arrest of anyone who dared mourn or view the body.

sentences.zh: 布還，奏事彭越頭下，祠而哭之。

translations.literal: Bu returned, reported his mission beneath Peng Yue's head, sacrificed, and wept for him.
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu came back, presented his report beneath Peng Yue's severed head, offered sacrifice, and wept.

sentences.zh: 吏捕以聞。

translations.literal: The officials arrested him and reported it.
translations.idiomatic: Officials seized him and reported the matter.

sentences.zh: 上召布罵曰：「若與彭越反邪？

translations.literal: The sovereign summoned Bu and reviled him: "Did you conspire with Peng Yue in rebellion?
translations.idiomatic: The emperor summoned Luan Bu and shouted, "Did you plot rebellion with Peng Yue?

sentences.zh: 吾禁人勿收，若獨祠而哭之，與反明矣。

translations.literal: I forbade people from collecting him; you alone sacrificed and wept for him—with the rebels it is already clear."
translations.idiomatic: I forbade anyone to mourn him; you alone held rites and wept—that marks you plainly as his accomplice."

sentences.zh: 趣亨之。」

translations.literal: Hasten to boil him."
translations.idiomatic: Boil him at once."

sentences.zh: 方提趨湯，顧曰：「願一言而死。」

translations.literal: Just as he was being seized and hurried toward the cauldron, he looked back and said: "I wish to speak one word and then die."
translations.idiomatic: As guards dragged him toward the boiling cauldron, he turned and said, "Let me speak one sentence before I die."

sentences.zh: 上曰：「何言？」

translations.literal: The sovereign said: "What words?"
translations.idiomatic: The emperor asked, "What is it?"

sentences.zh: 布曰：「方上之困彭城，敗滎陽、成皋間，項王所以不能遂西，徒以彭王居梁地，與漢合從苦楚也。

translations.literal: Bu said: "When Your Majesty was trapped at Pengcheng and defeated between Xingyang and Chenggao, the reason King Xiang could not press west was solely that King Peng held Liang territory and joined Han in a vertical alliance to exhaust Chu.
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu said, "When you were pinned at Pengcheng and beaten between Xingyang and Chenggao, the only reason Xiang Yu could not drive west was that King Peng held the Liang region in alliance with Han and wore Chu down.

sentences.zh: 當是之時，彭王壹顧，與楚則漢破，與漢則楚破。

translations.literal: At that time, if King Peng had turned his glance once—if with Chu then Han was broken, if with Han then Chu was broken.
translations.idiomatic: At that moment a single shift of Peng Yue's loyalty would have shattered Han had he joined Chu, or shattered Chu had he stayed with Han.

sentences.zh: 且垓下之會，微彭王，項氏不亡。

translations.literal: Moreover, at the meeting below Gaixia, without King Peng the Xiang clan would not have perished.
translations.idiomatic: At Gaixia, without King Peng, the house of Xiang would never have been destroyed.

sentences.zh: 天下已定，彭王剖符受封，亦欲傳之萬世。

translations.literal: When the realm was settled, King Peng received the tally and investiture and also wished to pass them on for ten thousand generations.
translations.idiomatic: Peace secured, King Peng took the bronze tally and hereditary fief, expecting them to pass to endless generations.

sentences.zh: 今漢壹徵兵於梁，彭王病不行，而疑以為反。

translations.literal: Now Han once summoned troops in Liang, and King Peng was ill and did not go, yet he was suspected of rebellion.
translations.idiomatic: Now a single levy in Liang found Peng Yue too ill to march, and you branded him a traitor for it.

sentences.zh: 反形未見，以苛細誅之，臣恐功臣人人自危也。

translations.literal: The form of rebellion had not yet appeared, yet he was executed on petty pretexts—I fear the meritorious ministers will each feel imperiled.
translations.idiomatic: No overt treason had appeared; you killed him on trifling suspicions—I fear every accomplished minister now sleeps uneasy.

sentences.zh: 今彭王已死，臣生不如死，請就亨。」

translations.literal: Now King Peng is dead; your servant's life is not worth keeping—please let me go to the boiling."
translations.idiomatic: Peng Yue is dead; I no longer care to live—send me to the cauldron."

sentences.zh: 上乃釋布，拜為都尉。

translations.literal: The sovereign then released Bu and appointed him commandant.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor spared him and named him a commandant.

sentences.zh: 孝文時，為燕相，至將軍。

translations.literal: In the time of Emperor Xiaowen he was chancellor of Yan, reaching the rank of general.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Xiaowen he rose from chancellor of Yan to general.

sentences.zh: 布稱曰：「窮困不能辱身，非人也；

translations.literal: Bu declared: "In poverty and straits, if one cannot endure humiliation to the body, one is not a human being;
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu used to say, "To endure want yet refuse to humble the body is to fall short of being human;

sentences.zh: 富貴不能快意，非賢也。」

translations.literal: in wealth and honor, if one cannot fulfill one's will, one is not worthy."
translations.idiomatic: to enjoy wealth and rank yet gain no satisfaction is to fall short of being worthy."

sentences.zh: 於是嘗有德，厚報之；

translations.literal: Therefore he who once showed him kindness he repaid generously;
translations.idiomatic: Those who had done him a kindness he rewarded lavishly;

sentences.zh: 有怨，必以法滅之。

translations.literal: those who bore a grudge he was sure to destroy by law.
translations.idiomatic: those who had crossed him he hunted down within the law.

sentences.zh: 吳楚反時，以功封為鄃侯，復為燕相。

translations.literal: When Wu and Chu rebelled, he was enfeoffed as marquis of Yu for merit and again became chancellor of Yan.
translations.idiomatic: During the revolt of Wu and Chu he earned the title Marquis of Yu and returned as Yan's chancellor.

sentences.zh: 燕齊之間皆為立社，號曰欒公社。

translations.literal: Between Yan and Qi they all established shrines for him, calling them the shrines of Lord Luan.
translations.idiomatic: From Yan to Qi the people built shrines to him and called them Lord Luan's altars.

sentences.zh: 布薨，子賁嗣侯，孝武時坐為太常犧牲不如令，國除。

translations.literal: When Bu died, his son Ben inherited the marquisate; in the time of Emperor Xiaowu he was deprived of his state for an offense in serving as grand libationer when the sacrificial victims did not conform to regulations.
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu died; his son Ben succeeded the fief, but under Emperor Wu he lost the title because, as grand libationer, the sacrificial beasts he presented failed to meet statute.

sentences.zh: 田叔，趙陘城人也。

translations.literal: Tian Shu was a man of Xingcheng in Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu came from Xingcheng in Zhao.

sentences.zh: 其先，齊田氏也。

translations.literal: His ancestors were the Tian clan of Qi.
translations.idiomatic: His forebears were the Tian lineage of Qi.

sentences.zh: 叔好劍，學黃老術於樂鉅公。

translations.literal: Shu loved the sword and studied Huang-Lao techniques with the Lord of Yue Ju.
translations.idiomatic: He loved swordsmanship and studied Huang-Lao doctrine under Yue Ju Gong.

sentences.zh: 為人廉直，喜任俠。

translations.literal: In conduct he was incorrupt and upright, and delighted in knight-errantry.
translations.idiomatic: He was honest and blunt by nature and relished the knight-errant's code.

sentences.zh: 游諸公，趙人舉之趙相趙午，言之趙王張敖，以為郎中。

translations.literal: He traveled among the great men; men of Zhao recommended him to Chancellor Zhao Wu of Zhao, who spoke of him to King Zhang Ao of Zhao, and he was appointed gentleman of the palace.
translations.idiomatic: He moved in elite circles until Zhao men recommended him to Chancellor Zhao Wu, who introduced him to King Zhang Ao of Zhao, who made him a gentleman of the palace.

sentences.zh: 數歲，趙王賢之，未及遷。

translations.literal: After several years the king considered him worthy but had not yet promoted him.
translations.idiomatic: For years the king admired him yet never advanced his rank.

sentences.zh: 會趙午、貫高等謀弒上，事發覺，漢下詔捕趙王及群臣反者。

translations.literal: It happened that Zhao Wu, Guan Gao, and others plotted to assassinate the sovereign; when the affair was discovered, Han issued an edict to arrest the king of Zhao and the ministers who had rebelled.
translations.idiomatic: Then Zhao Wu, Guan Gao, and others conspired to murder the emperor; when the plot surfaced, the Han court ordered the arrest of the king of Zhao and every minister implicated in treason.

sentences.zh: 趙有敢隨王，罪三族。

translations.literal: Zhao issued that whoever dared follow the king would be guilty of a crime punishable by three clans.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao proclaimed that anyone who dared follow their king would face the three-clans penalty.

sentences.zh: 唯田叔、孟舒等十餘人赭衣自髡鉗，隨王至長安。

translations.literal: Only Tian Shu, Meng Shu, and more than ten others in crimson clothes shaved their own heads and put on the iron collar, and followed the king to Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: Only Tian Shu, Meng Shu, and a dozen others shaved their heads, donned the cangue and crimson convict dress, and followed their king to Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 趙王敖事白，得出，廢王為宣平侯，乃進言叔等十人。

translations.literal: The affair of King Ao of Zhao was cleared; he was able to leave, was demoted from king to Marquis of Xuanping, and then advanced word on Tian Shu and the ten men.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Ao's name was cleared; he was released, stripped of his kingship and enfeoffed as Marquis of Xuanping, and then recommended Tian Shu and the other nine men to the throne.

sentences.zh: 上召見，與語，漢廷臣無能出其右者。

translations.literal: The sovereign summoned them to audience, conversed with them, and among Han court ministers none could surpass them.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor received them in audience and found that no minister at his court could match their quality.

sentences.zh: 上說，盡拜為郡守、諸侯相。

translations.literal: The sovereign was pleased and appointed them all governors and chancellors of feudal states.
translations.idiomatic: Delighted, he appointed each of them governor of a commandery or chancellor to a vassal kingdom.

sentences.zh: 叔為漢中守十餘年。

translations.literal: Shu was governor of Hanzhong for more than ten years.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu governed Hanzhong for over a decade.

sentences.zh: 孝文帝初立，召叔問曰：「公知天下長者乎？」

translations.literal: When Emperor Xiaowen had just ascended, he summoned Shu and asked: "Do you know who in the realm are men of true stature?"
translations.idiomatic: Early in Emperor Xiaowen's reign he called Tian Shu in and asked, "Who in the empire would you call a genuinely honorable man?"

sentences.zh: 對曰：「臣何足以知之！」

translations.literal: He replied: "How could your servant be fit to know such!"
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu answered, "How should a man like me presume to judge that?"

sentences.zh: 上曰：「公長者，宜知之。」

translations.literal: The sovereign said: "You are a man of stature; you ought to know."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, "You are such a man yourself; you must have an opinion."

sentences.zh: 叔頓首曰：「故雲中守孟舒，長者也。」

translations.literal: Shu knocked his head and said: "The former governor of Yunzhong, Meng Shu, is a man of stature."
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu kowtowed and named Meng Shu, the former governor of Yunzhong.

sentences.zh: 是時孟舒坐虜大入雲中免。

translations.literal: At that time Meng Shu sat because the barbarians had entered Yunzhong in great force and was dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: Meng Shu had just been removed from office for allowing a major Xiongnu raid into Yunzhong.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「先帝置孟舒雲中十餘年矣，虜常一入，孟舒不能堅守，無故士卒戰死者數百人。

translations.literal: The sovereign said: "The late emperor established Meng Shu at Yunzhong for more than ten years; the barbarians often entered once, and Meng Shu could not hold firm—without cause several hundred soldiers died in battle.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor objected: "The late emperor kept Meng Shu at Yunzhong for years; the raiders broke in once, he failed to hold the line, and hundreds of troops died for no good reason.

sentences.zh: 長者固殺人乎？」

translations.literal: Do men of stature by nature kill people?"
translations.idiomatic: Is that what you call honorable conduct—to let hundreds of men die?"

sentences.zh: 叔叩頭曰：「夫貫高等謀反，天子下明詔，趙有敢隨張王者罪三族，然孟舒自髡鉗，隨張王，以身死之，豈自知為雲中守哉！

translations.literal: Shu knocked his head and said: "As for Guan Gao and the others plotting rebellion, the Son of Heaven issued a clear edict, and Zhao had it that whoever dared follow King Zhang would be guilty of a crime punishable by three clans—yet Meng Shu shaved his own head and put on the collar and followed King Zhang, intending to die for him; how could he have known he would become governor of Yunzhong!
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu kowtowed again: "When Guan Gao plotted treason, the Son of Heaven issued an explicit decree: in Zhao, anyone who followed King Zhang Ao would face the three-clans penalty. Meng Shu still shaved his head, clamped himself in the cangue, and followed his king, ready to die with him—he could not have dreamed he would one day govern Yunzhong!

sentences.zh: 漢與楚相距，士卒罷敝，而匈奴冒頓新服北夷，來為邊寇，孟舒知士卒罷敝，不忍出言，士爭臨城死敵，如子為父，以故死者數百人，孟舒豈敺之哉！

translations.literal: Han and Chu faced each other; the soldiers were weary and worn, while the Xiongnu Modu had newly brought the northern tribes to submission and came raiding the border. Meng Shu knew the soldiers were weary and worn and could not bear to speak; the knights vied to go to the walls and die against the enemy like sons for a father—therefore several hundred died—how could Meng Shu have driven them!
translations.idiomatic: Han and Chu were locked in stalemate and the troops were exhausted, while Modu had just united the northern tribes and struck the frontier. Meng Shu saw how spent his men were and would not order them out; they rushed to the ramparts and died like sons for a father, so hundreds fell—Meng Shu did not drive them to it!

sentences.zh: 是乃孟舒所以為長者。」

translations.literal: This is why Meng Shu is a man of stature."
translations.idiomatic: That is the very proof that he is a man of honor."

sentences.zh: 於是上曰：「賢哉孟舒！」

translations.literal: Thereupon the sovereign said: "Worthy indeed is Meng Shu!"
translations.idiomatic: The emperor exclaimed, "Meng Shu is a true worthy!"

sentences.zh: 復召以為雲中守。

translations.literal: He summoned him again and made him governor of Yunzhong.
translations.idiomatic: He recalled Meng Shu and restored him as governor of Yunzhong.

sentences.zh: 後數歲，叔坐法失官。

translations.literal: Several years later Shu sat because of the law and lost office.
translations.idiomatic: Some years afterward Tian Shu lost his post over a legal infraction.

sentences.zh: 梁孝王使人殺漢議臣爰盎，景帝召叔案梁，具得其事。

translations.literal: King Xiao of Liang had someone kill the Han counselor Yuan Ang; Emperor Jing summoned Shu to investigate Liang and he obtained the full facts.
translations.idiomatic: King Liu Wu of Liang had the Han adviser Yuan Ang murdered; Emperor Jing sent Tian Shu to investigate the kingdom, and he uncovered the whole affair.

sentences.zh: 還報，上曰：「梁有之乎？」

translations.literal: Returning to report, the sovereign said: "Did Liang do it?"
translations.idiomatic: When he reported back, the emperor asked bluntly, "Did Liang do it?"

sentences.zh: 對曰：「有之。」

translations.literal: He replied: "It did."
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu said, "Yes."

sentences.zh: 「事安在？」

translations.literal: "Where is the matter?"
translations.idiomatic: "Where is the proof?"

sentences.zh: 叔曰：「上無以梁事為問也。

translations.literal: Shu said: "Your Majesty should not ask about Liang's affair.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu replied, "Your Majesty must not press me for the details of Liang's guilt.

sentences.zh: 今梁王不伏誅，是廢漢法也；

translations.literal: If now the king of Liang is not executed, this is casting aside Han law;
translations.idiomatic: If the king of Liang goes unpunished, the laws of Han are meaningless;

sentences.zh: 如其伏誅，太后食不甘味，臥不安席，此憂在陛下。」

translations.literal: if he is executed, the empress dowager will not find her food sweet nor her couch peaceful—this sorrow will lie on Your Majesty."
translations.idiomatic: if he is executed, the empress dowager will lose her appetite and her sleep—the grief will be yours to bear."

sentences.zh: 於是上大賢之，以為魯相。

translations.literal: Thereupon the sovereign greatly deemed him worthy and made him chancellor of Lu.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor admired his candor and named him chancellor of Lu.

sentences.zh: 相初至官，民以王取其財物自言者百餘人。

translations.literal: When the chancellor first arrived at his post, commoners who on their own spoke of the king having seized their goods were more than a hundred.
translations.idiomatic: Soon after he took office, well over a hundred people petitioned that the king of Lu had seized their property.

sentences.zh: 叔取其渠率二千人笞，怒之曰：「王非汝主邪？

translations.literal: Tian Shu took their ringleaders, two thousand men, and flogged them, angrily saying to them: "Is not the king your master?
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu seized some two thousand ringleaders, had them flogged, and demanded: "Is the king of Lu not your master?

sentences.zh: 何敢自言主！」

translations.literal: How dare you speak on your own of your master!"
translations.idiomatic: How dare you bring a public complaint against your own king!"

sentences.zh: 魯王聞之，大慚，發中府錢，使相償之。

translations.literal: When the king of Lu heard, he was greatly ashamed, opened the middle treasury's cash, and had the chancellor repay them.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Lu was mortified, opened the inner treasury, and sent funds for the chancellor to repay them.

sentences.zh: 相曰：「王自使人償之，不爾，是王為惡而相為善也。」

translations.literal: The chancellor said: "Let the king himself send men to repay them; if not, this is the king doing evil and the chancellor doing good."
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu refused: "Let His Highness dispatch his own agents to make restitution; otherwise it would look as though the king had done wrong and the chancellor had bought virtue."

sentences.zh: 魯王好獵，相常從入苑中，王輒休相就館。

translations.literal: The king of Lu loved hunting; the chancellor often followed him into the park, and the king always rested the chancellor and had him go to the lodge.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Lu loved the chase; the chancellor usually accompanied him into the hunting park, and the king always urged him to retire to the guest quarters.

sentences.zh: 相常暴坐苑外，終不休，曰：「吾王暴露，獨何為舍？」

translations.literal: The chancellor often sat exposed outside the park and to the end did not rest, saying: "Our king is exposed to wind and dew—why alone should I take shelter?"
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu instead sat in the open outside the enclosure and refused to leave, saying, "While my lord endures the elements, how may I seek shelter?"

sentences.zh: 王以故不大出遊。

translations.literal: For this reason the king did not much go out roaming.
translations.idiomatic: The king therefore curtailed his excursions.

sentences.zh: 數年以官卒，魯以百金祠，少子仁不受，曰：「義不傷先人名。」

translations.literal: Several years later he died in office; Lu offered a hundred catties of gold for sacrifice, but the youngest son Ren would not accept, saying: "In righteousness I will not harm my forebear's name."
translations.idiomatic: He died in office a few years later. Lu offered a hundred pounds of gold for his rites, but his youngest son Tian Ren declined, declaring, "Duty forbids me to trade on my father's reputation."

sentences.zh: 仁以壯勇為衛將軍舍人，數從擊匈奴。

translations.literal: Ren by stalwart courage became a retainer of the General of Guards and several times followed in striking the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Ren, bold in battle, entered the household of the General of Guards as a retainer and repeatedly campaigned against the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 衛將軍進言仁為郎中，至二千石、丞相長史，失官。

translations.literal: The General of Guards advanced word on Ren as gentleman of the palace; he reached two thousand bushels and chief clerk to the chancellor, then lost office.
translations.idiomatic: The general recommended him as a gentleman of the palace; he rose to two thousand bushels and chief clerk under the chancellor, then lost his post.

sentences.zh: 後使刺三河，還，奏事稱意，拜為京輔都尉。

translations.literal: Later he was sent to investigate the Three He; returning, his memorial pleased the throne, and he was appointed metropolitan commandant.
translations.idiomatic: Later he was dispatched to inspect the Three He region; his report satisfied the throne, and he was named metropolitan commandant.

sentences.zh: 月餘，遷司直。

translations.literal: After more than a month he was promoted to director of correctness.
translations.idiomatic: Within a month he was advanced to director of correctness.

sentences.zh: 數歲，戾太子舉兵，仁部閉城門，令太子得亡，坐縱反者族。

translations.literal: Several years later the heir apparent Li raised troops; Ren's division closed the city gates yet ordered that the heir apparent be allowed to escape, and for letting the rebel go he was liable to execution of the clan.
translations.idiomatic: When Prince Li rebelled, Tian Ren's unit sealed the gates but left the heir apparent a path to flee; he was condemned for releasing a traitor and his whole clan faced extinction.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：以項羽之氣，而季布以勇顯名楚，身履軍搴旗者數矣，可謂壯士。

translations.literal: The appraisal says: With Xiang Yu's spirit, yet Ji Bu by courage displayed a famous name in Chu; personally he trod the army and tore enemy banners several times—he may be called a stalwart.
translations.idiomatic: The historian's verdict: For all of Xiang Yu's towering pride, Ji Bu won renown in Chu by sheer valor, time and again charging the line and snatching enemy banners—a true fighting man.

sentences.zh: 及至困厄奴僇，苟活而不變，何也？

translations.literal: When it came to being trapped and enslaved, he clung to life without changing—why?
translations.idiomatic: Yet when ruin reduced him to slavery, he endured humiliation rather than die—why?

sentences.zh: 彼自負其材，受辱不羞，欲有所用其未足也，故終為漢名將。

translations.literal: He trusted his own talent, received insult without shame, wishing to employ what was not yet exhausted in him—therefore in the end he became a famous general of Han.
translations.idiomatic: He trusted his abilities, bore disgrace without flinching, and waited until he could put his powers to full use—hence he ended his days as one of Han's celebrated generals.

sentences.zh: 賢者誠重其死。

translations.literal: The worthy truly weigh their deaths.
translations.idiomatic: A worthy man weighs carefully how he meets his end.

sentences.zh: 夫婢妾賤人，感概而自殺，非能勇也，其畫無俚之至耳。

translations.literal: Slave girls and mean persons, stirred in feeling and killing themselves—this is not being able to be brave; their plans reach the utmost of vulgarity.
translations.idiomatic: Maids and base folk who take offense and cut their own throats show no courage—only the shallowest desperation.

sentences.zh: 欒布哭彭越，田叔隨張敖，赴死如歸，彼誠知所處，雖古烈士，何以加哉！

translations.literal: Luan Bu wept for Peng Yue; Tian Shu followed Zhang Ao—they went to death as if returning home; they truly knew where they stood—even the ancient martyrs—how could they be surpassed!
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu mourned Peng Yue; Tian Shu followed Zhang Ao to the scaffold as calmly as a man going home. They knew exactly what they stood for—no hero of antiquity could surpass them.
